coredata.dc:title: Iron and copper codoped carbon nanodots as oxidase mimics and fluorescent probes for detection of phenol and dimethoate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Weidong
Tian, Hongyuan
Feng, Xinyi
Tang, Zhanqiu
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nanozymes represent a class of dual-functional nanomaterials that exhibit inherent enzyme-like catalytic properties alongside fluorescent emission, making them suitable for multiple detection applications. However, the number of publications on this subject is limited, primarily due to potential interference between the two processes. In this study, oxidase-like fluorescent iron and copper codoped carbon dots (Fe,Cu-CDs) were synthesized via a one-pot pyrolysis reaction. The results demonstrated that the carbon dots could catalyze the oxidative coupling reaction between phenol and 4-aminoantipyrine, while their fluorescence emission was enhanced upon coordination with thiol compounds. By integrating the dimethoate-mediated, acetylcholinesterase-catalyzed hydrolysis of thiocholine, a dual-detection platform was developed for the colorimetric detection of phenol and the fluorometric detection of dimethoate, achieving detection limits of 0.103 μM and 1.94 × 10−5 µg/mL, respectively. This method was subsequently applied to detect these two pollutants in real water and vegetable samples, and the results demonstrated favorable recovery rates and good reproducibility. This work not only presents a novel strategy for the synthesis and application of dual-functional carbon dots in pollutant analysis but also offers new insights into the design of dual-functional carbon dots for efficient and cost-effective multidetection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Oxidase mimics
Phenol
Dimethoate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of the Fe/Cu-CD based sensing platform
Figures of merits of the method

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM characterization of the Fe,Cu-CDs: HRTEM image (A); histogram of the size distribution (B); FT-IR spectra of the Fe,Cu-CDs (C); XPS high-resolution survey scan of N 1 s (D), Fe 2p (E) and Cu 2p (F).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of DHE, Fe,Cu-CDs, and DHE + Fe,Cu-CDs after incubation at 37 °C for 30 min; (B) UV–vis spectra of TMB, Fe,Cu-CDs, and TMB + Fe,Cu-CDs after incubation at 37 °C for 30 min; (C) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm of the TMB/Fe,Cu-CD system at different TMB concentrations (0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and 0.30 mM); (D) UV–vis spectra of phenol, 4-AAP, Fe,Cu-CDs, 4-AAP + phenol, 4-AAP + Fe,Cu-CDs, phenol + Fe,Cu-CDs, and 4-AAP + phenol + Fe,Cu-CDs after incubation at 37 °C for 30 min.
Optimization of experimental conditions for phenol detection. (A) Buffer pH; (B) incubation temperature; (C) the concentration of Fe,Cu-CDs; (D) the concentration of 4-AAP.

(A) Representative UV–vis spectra and images of the sensing system with different concentrations of phenol; (B) the linear relationship between ΔA and the concentrations of phenol; (C) representative fluorescence spectra of the sensing system with different concentrations of dimethoate; (D) the linear relationship between ΔF and the logarithm of dimethoate concentration. Error bars were the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

Influence of phenol on the fluorescent emission of the Fe,Cu-Cds (A) and the fluorescent response of 0.1 µg/mL dimethoate (B); selectivity of the platform for the phenol (C) and dimethoate (D) detection.
Mechanisms of the dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric assay for phenol and dimethoate detection with Fe,Cu-CDs.
Detection of phenol in real samples (n = 3).
Detection of dimethoate in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Iron and copper codoped carbon nanodots as oxidase mimics and fluorescent probes for detection of phenol and dimethoate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nanozymes represent a class of dual-functional nanomaterials that exhibit inherent enzyme-like catalytic properties alongside fluorescent emission, making them suitable for multiple detection applications. However, the number of publications on this subject is limited, primarily due to potential interference between the two processes. In this study, oxidase-like fluorescent iron and copper codoped carbon dots (Fe,Cu-CDs) were synthesized via a one-pot pyrolysis reaction. The results demonstrated that the carbon dots could catalyze the oxidative coupling reaction between phenol and 4-aminoantipyrine, while their fluorescence emission was enhanced upon coordination with thiol compounds. By integrating the dimethoate-mediated, acetylcholinesterase-catalyzed hydrolysis of thiocholine, a dual-detection platform was developed for the colorimetric detection of phenol and the fluorometric detection of dimethoate, achieving detection limits of 0.103 μM and 1.94 × 10−5 µg/mL, respectively. This method was subsequently applied to detect these two pollutants in real water and vegetable samples, and the results demonstrated favorable recovery rates and good reproducibility. This work not only presents a novel strategy for the synthesis and application of dual-functional carbon dots in pollutant analysis but also offers new insights into the design of dual-functional carbon dots for efficient and cost-effective multidetection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenols are a category of prevalent water pollutants that are discharged from chemical manufacturing facilities, pharmaceutical plants, and various sectors of the petrochemical industry [1]. These compounds have become widely disseminated in the environment, posing significant risks to both ecosystems and living organisms. Phenolic compounds are associated with neurotoxicity, carcinogenicity, and developmental and behavioral disorders. Similarly, organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) represent another class of environmental pollutants that have been extensively utilized to manage insect populations in agricultural regions worldwide due to their cost-effectiveness and high insecticidal efficacy. However, improper application and disposal of OPs can lead to severe contamination of surface water and groundwater, as well as agricultural products [2,3]. OPs function by inhibiting the catalytic activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), thereby disrupting neurotransmitter transmission and ultimately resulting in mortality. Consequently, the development of reliable techniques for the rapid and sensitive detection of phenols and OPs in environmental and food matrices is of paramount importance.

Numerous traditional techniques have been developed to detect phenols and OPs, such as liquid chromatography, gas chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, colorimetry, fluorometry and electrochemical analysis [4–6]. Although chromatography and capillary electrophoresis provide high selectivity and good sensitivity, they necessitate expensive equipment and highly trained personnel. Colorimetry, fluorometry and electrochemical analysis can provide quick analysis. However, very few publications can be found for the simultaneous detection of these two kinds of the pollutants [7].

Nanozymes are enzyme-like nanomaterials that serve as cost-effective alternatives to natural enzymes due to their tunable catalytic activity and exceptional stability under harsh conditions [8]. Moreover, it has been reported that bimetallic-doped nanozymes exhibit superior antibacterial efficacy and possess a broader range of properties compared to their monometallic counterparts [9,10]. Recently, we discovered that fluorescent iron and copper codoped carbon dots (Fe,Cu-CDs) could function as oxidases, catalyzing the oxidative coupling of 4-aminoantipyrine and phenol to produce quinoneimine, which exhibited strong characteristic absorbance. Notably, after the catalytic reaction, the Fe,Cu-CDs could quantitatively bind thiocholine (TCh), resulting in a concentration-dependent increase of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1, panels B and D). On the contrary, the copper-doped CDs (Cu-CDs), a well-known oxidase-like nanozyme, did not response significantly to TCh at concentrations less than 150 µM (Fig. S1, A and C). The results suggest that, compared to the monodoped Cu-CDs, Fe,Cu-CDs serve as an effective fluorescence probe for TCh. It is well known that AChE can react with thiocholine chloride (ATCh) to produce small molecules containing thiol groups [11]. In contrast, OPs can strongly bind to AChE, resulting in the formation of chemically stable phosphorylated compounds that inhibit the enzymatic activity of AChE [12]. Phenol and dimethoate, which belong to the phenolic and organophosphorus categories, have raised increasing concern over the past few decades, prompting numerous countries and organizations to establish regulations regarding their maximum contamination levels. Inspired by published reports and findings, we propose a sensing platform based on Fe,Cu-CDs for the colorimetric and fluorescent detection of these pollutants. This method enables rapid detection of both pollutants with significantly low detection limits, excellent linearity, and high selectivity.

Fluorescent carbon dot-based nanozymes exhibit dual functionality. On one hand, they function as fluorescent probes [13–19]; on the other hand, they act as catalysts for specific reactions. Consequently, they offer promising applications in multi-detection systems. However, employing these nanomaterials for multiple detection poses a significant challenge, as the processes involved in fluorometric detection and catalysis may interfere with one another. For instance, the properties of metal-doped nanozymes can change after catalysis, primarily due to alterations in the oxidation state of the metal during the catalytic process, as well as the potential coordination of metal ions with substrates or products. These changes may subsequently impact the fluorescence characteristics of the nanozymes. Conversely, when employed as fluorescent probes, the interaction of CDs with various stimuli may influence their catalytic capabilities [20]. This study contributes to our understanding of the design and application of nanozymes for multiple detection, thereby potentially advancing their use in the sensitive and selective detection of environmental pollutants.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid monosodium ferric salt (EDTA-FeNa), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid copper disodium salt (EDTA-CuNa2), acetonitrile, methanol, ethanol, 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AAP), cyclohexane, acetone, dimethoate, trimethylaminomethane, sodium chloride, dihydroethidium (DHE), acetylthiocholine chloride (ATCh), and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) were purchased from Mecklin Biochemical (Shanghai, China). Sodium carbonate, sodium nitrate, sodium sulfate, hydrochloric acid, acetic acid, and potassium chloride were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagents (Beijing, China). Phenol was obtained from Bodi Chemicals (Tianjin, China). Calcium chloride and magnesium chloride were products of Chengnan Chemicals (Zhejiang, China). Glycine, cysteine, and methionine were obtained from Ruji Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Sarcosine was from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical reagent grade and were used without any purification. All solutions were prepared using triple-distilled water.

The synthesis of Fe,Cu-carbon dots (CDs) was conducted according to a previously established protocol [21] with modifications. In brief, 2 g of EDTA-FeNa and 2 g of EDTA-CuNa2 were dissolved in 20 mL of triple-distilled water, followed by one hour of ultrasonic treatment. The resulting solution was then vacuum-dried overnight at 70 °C. The dried product was subsequently transferred to a quartz boat and placed in a tube furnace. The temperature was increased at a rate of 5 ℃/minute under a nitrogen atmosphere and maintained at 300 °C for 2 h. After this process, the products were collected and dispersed in 10 mL of 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, stirred at 60 °C for 8 h, and then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm to remove larger particles. The resulting suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter (Jiuding High Tech, Beijing, China) and vacuum-dried at 60 °C overnight. The CDs were subsequently collected and stored at 4 °C prior to use.
For comparison, Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) were synthesized using 4 g of EDTA-CuNa2, following the same procedures described above.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the Fe,Cu-CDs were captured using a Talos F200S transmission electron microscope (FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out on an IRAffinity-1 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) study was conducted on an ESCSLAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Leicestershire, UK) equipped with Al-Ka radiation. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were conducted using an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK). The quantum yields (QYs) were measured using a C11347-11 Quantaurus-QY absolute photoluminescence quantum yield spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan).

The water samples collected for analysis included tap water from the laboratory and river water from the Beihucheng River in Beijing, China. Initially, these samples were filtered through 0.45 μm membranes (Jiuding High Tech, Beijing, China) to remove larger suspended particles. Subsequently, the water samples were independently spiked with varying concentrations of phenol (5, 25, and 50 µM) and dimethoate (0.05, 0.10, and 0.15 µg/mL) for further analysis.

The vegetable samples of cabbage and garlic chives were purchased from a local supermarket and treated according to the procedures outlined in reference [22], with minor modifications. Briefly, each sample (2 g) was washed with distilled water and subsequently homogenized. To each homogenate, 1 mL of triple-distilled water was added, along with varying concentrations of dimethoate at 0.05, 0.1, and 0.15 µg/mL. After an 8-hour storage period at 4 °C, 5 mL of methanol was introduced, and the mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. The resulting suspension was centrifuged at 16,000 g for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected and vacuum-dried at 35 °C. The residue was then dissolved in 2.0 mL of 0.2 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.8) prior to analysis.

A 200 µL aliquot of the phenol standard or sample (referred to as the test solution) was combined with 50 µL of 0.2 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.8), 20 µL of 2 mM 4-AAP, and 50 µL of Fe,Cu-CDs at a concentration of 0.3 mg/mL. The resulting mixture was thoroughly vortexed and subsequently incubated at 70 °C for 30 min to obtain Solution A, which was then analyzed using UV–visible spectrophotometry. For the blank measurement, the test solution was replaced with triple-distilled water. The change in absorbance (ΔA) at 502 nm, resulting from the comparison of the test solution to the blank, was used as the index for phenol detection.

A 10 µL aliquot of dimethoate standard or sample (referred to as the test solution) was mixed thoroughly with 50 µL of 0.2 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.8) and 50 µL of 50 mU/mL AChE, then was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Afterward, 50 µL of 75 µM ATCh was added, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for another 30 min to obtain Solution B, which was subsequently combined with Solution A and incubated at 37 °C for another 30 min. The resulting mixture was excited at a wavelength of 350 nm, and the fluorescence was measured at 450 nm. For the blank measurement, the test solution was replaced with triple-distilled water. The luminescence change ΔF, resulting from the comparison of the test solution to the blank, was employed for quantitative analysis.

The morphology of the as-prepared Fe,Cu-CDs was characterized by HRTEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the CDs were approximately spherical particles. The size distribution of the particles predominantly ranged from 2.0 to 6.0 nm (Fig. 1B), with an average diameter of 4.0 nm. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. S2) clearly displays a lattice spacing of approximately 0.37 nm, indicating the presence of the (002) facet of graphite [23].

FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1C) was used to study the functional groups on the surface of the CDs. The absorption peaks at 3404 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, 1381 cm−1 and 1107 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO, CN and CO groups, respectively. These results suggest the existence of N-containing groups and O-containing groups on the surface of the CDs. The peak at 1041 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of N-Cu [24], whereas the peaks at 580 cm−1 and 820 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Fe–N and Fe–OH, respectively [25].

XPS was employed to investigate the chemical composition and surface states of the Fe,Cu-CDs. The full range XPS spectrum (Fig. S3) revealed that the contents of Fe and Cu were 2.33 % and 2.39 %, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum showed three peaks at 398.4, 399.1 and 400.9 eV (Fig. 1D), which correspond to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N/N–H, respectively [26]. Pyridine and pyrrole nitrogen can form M-Nx coordination bonds with metals, and may become active sites for the catalytic reactions [27]. In the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1E), the Fe species existed in six types: 710.07 eV for Fe2+ 2p3/2, 712.47 eV for Fe3+ 2p3/2, 722.32 eV for Fe2+ 2p1/2, 724.87 eV for Fe3+ 2p1/2, and two satellite peaks at 717.32 eV and 729.07 eV for Fe3+ 2p3/2 and Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively. The results are similar to those of previous reports [28]. In Fig. 1F, the peaks at 931.57, 933.32, 951.17, and 953.42 eV were attributed to Cu+ 2p3/2, Cu2+ 2p3/2, Cu+ 2p1/2, and Cu2+ 2p12, respectively, whereas the peaks in the regions of 936 ∼ 945 eV and 957 ∼ 965 eV were satellite peaks [29].

The above results suggest that the Fe and Cu codoped carbon nanodots were successfully synthesized. The surfaces of the CDs were rich in hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups, which contributed to their high water solubility and good coordination ability to the metal ions. Further, considerable portions of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were reduced to Fe2+ and Cu+, respectively. The coexistence of these metal species at different valences is expected to improve the surface chemical reactivities of the CDs.

One notable characteristic of oxidase enzymes is their ability to catalyze the conversion of dissolved O2 to superoxide radicals (O2
−). The oxidase-mimetic properties of the synthesized Fe,Cu-CDs were verified using DHE, a commonly used probe for O2
−. In the presence of O2
−, DHE undergoes a two-electron oxidation process, resulting in the formation of fluorescent ethidium. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, in the absence of DHE, the incubation of Fe,Cu-CDs solutions did not produce any detectable fluorescent emission at 610 nm, and the emission intensity of the DHE solution was notably weak. However, incubation of the Fe,Cu-CD/DHE mixture led to significant fluorescent emission upon excitation at 520 nm. The results suggest that the CDs are oxidase mimics.

To quantitatively evaluate the oxidase-like activity of the CDs, TMB was employed as the chromogenic substrate. In the presence of CD oxidase mimics, TMB undergoes oxidation, resulting in the formation of a blue product, oxTMB, which exhibits a new absorption band centered at 652 nm (Fig. 2B), and significantly higher baseline at 750 nm when comparing the spectra obtained with TMB and Fe,Cu-CDs. The surfaces of the CDs were rich in π-bonds [30], which could interact with TMB and oxTMB molecules through non-covalent π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, facilitating adsorption of these molecules to Fe,Cu-CDs. Furthermore, the adsorption process could significantly alter the UV–vis absorbance properties of TMB and oxTMB [31]. Fig. 2B suggests that the interaction between CDs and oxTMB leads to enhanced absorbance at 750 nm. At the wavelength of 652 nm, the time-dependent changes in the absorbance of the reaction solution were monitored (Fig. 2C). The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax) were determined to be 1.25 mM and 2.90 × 10−8 M·s−1, respectively, which were similar or better than the previous reports [32]. The low Km and high Vmax values suggested that the Fe,Cu-CDs possessed good catalytic capacities.

As illustrated in Fig. 2D, solutions containing phenol, 4-AAP, and Fe,Cu-CDs, along with their various combinations—4-AAP + phenol, 4-AAP + Fe,Cu-CDs, phenol + Fe,Cu-CDs, and 4-AAP + phenol + Fe,Cu-CDs—were incubated and subsequently tested for absorbance within the wavelength range of 400–700 nm. In contrast to conditions where no significant absorbance was detected, a pronounced absorption peak at 502 nm was observed upon the addition of CDs to the 4-AAP + phenol system. These findings suggest that the Fe,Cu-CDs may function as an oxidase. The colorimetric reaction was initiated by the decomposition of O2 to O2
− on the surfaces of Fe,Cu-CDs, followed by a catalytic oxidation, which transforms phenol into phenolic hydroxyl radicals. The resulting product further reacted with 4-AAP, producing a pink-colored quinoneimine. The bimetallic-doped CDs exhibited good oxidase-like catalytic activity, facilitating the coupling reaction between phenol and 4-AAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the Fe/Cu-CD based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The absolute QY for the carbon dots was determined to be 1.9 %, which is comparable to the values reported by other researchers for carbon dots [33,34]. In the fluorescence detection system, ATCh acts as a substrate that is converted into thiocholine (TCh) through the catalysis of AChE. Additionally, TCh could coordinate with Cu/Fe of the Fe,Cu-CDs via sulfhydryl groups (−SH). As illustrated in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of Fe,Cu-CDs increased nearly linearly with the addition of TCh. However, when AChE is pretreated with organophosphorus pesticides, its enzymatic activity is inhibited, leading to a decrease in the generation rate of TCh and, consequently, impeding the fluorescence enhancement of the CDs.

Scheme 1 illustrates the mechanism underlying the colorimetric detection of phenol and the fluorometric detection of dimethoate using the as-synthesized Fe,Cu-CDs. The nanomaterials function as oxidase mimetics, catalyzing the oxidative coupling of phenol with 4-AAP to produce a red product with a characteristic absorption peak at 502 nm. Consequently, the change in the absorbance of the system can be employed for the quantitative detection of phenol.

Due to their abundance and diverse applications, phenolic compounds have been the subject of extensive research regarding their chemical properties and the methodologies employed for their detection [35–45]. In this work, detection of phenol was achieved through an oxidative coupling reaction catalyzed by Fe,Cu-CDs. To improve the detection performance of this method, we systematically examined the effects of buffer pH, reaction temperature, and the concentrations of CDs and 4-AAP.

The reaction between 4-AAP and phenol is sensitive to pH variations [46]. Our results (Fig. 3 A) revealed that within the pH range of 1.0–9.0, the maximum signal intensity was achieved at pH 7.8. Therefore, a pH of 7.8 was employed for the subsequent studies. The effect of the derivatization reaction temperature was studied by varying the temperature of the water bath from 37 ℃ to 80 ℃. Fig. 3B indicates that the response signal (ΔA) increased with increasing temperature, reaching a maximum at 70 ℃. A further increase in the temperature resulted in fading of the color. Our results suggest that increasing the temperature promoted the reaction between phenol and 4-AAP, and that oxidization of 4-AAP might occur at high temperatures [47]. Therefore, the derivatization reaction was carried out in a water bath at 70 ℃. As a nanozyme, the concentration of CDs influences the response signal intensity of the chromogenic system. Fig. 3C depicts that the ΔA value increased with the increasing concentration of the Fe,Cu-CDs, ultimately reaching saturation at 0.3 mg/mL. As seen in Fig. 3D, increasing the concentration of 4-AAP in solution enhanced the response signal up to a concentration of 2.0 mM, after which the sensitivity decreased progressively. High 4-AAP concentration promoted the coupling reaction with phenol. However, under the experimental conditions, an excess of 4-AAP might undergo oxidation, potentially leading to increased background absorbance [48]. Consequently, a concentration of 2 mM 4-AAP in buffer was selected for the experiment.
The parameters for the fluorometric detection of dimethoate were also optimized. For further details, please refer to Fig. S4, Fig. S5, Fig. S6 and Fig. S7 in the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Figures of merits of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the analytical performance of the sensing platform was thoroughly investigated. For the detection of phenol, Fig. 4 A illustrates that the absorbance at 502 nm increased progressively with the concentration of the analyte. Correspondingly, a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.9973) was established between the relative peak height (ΔA) and the phenol concentration within the range of 0.5 to 100 μM (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.103 μM based on 3σ/S, where σ represents the relative standard deviation of the absorbance of the blank (n = 11) and S denotes the slope of the calibration plot. Notably, from a phenol concentration of 5 μM onward, the color change resulting from the chromogenic reaction was discernable with the naked eye (Fig. 4A, inset).

The sensitive fluorescence detection of dimethoate was also realized. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm of the sensing system progressively decreased with increasing concentrations of dimethoate. Moreover, Fig. 4D indicates that a good correlation (R2 = 0.9907) exists between ΔF and the logarithm of the dimethoate concentration within the range of 1 × 10−4 and 5 µg/mL, with a LOD of 1.94 × 10−5 µg/mL. Dimethoate is classified as a relatively weak inhibitor of acetylcholinesterase within the organophosphorus pesticide family [49]. Furthermore, it undergoes rapid decomposition in neutral to alkaline aqueous solutions [50]. These characteristics may significantly impact the detection limit of our proposed method. Nonetheless, it is worth mentioning that the LODs for phenol and dimethoate obtained by our Fe,Cu-CD-based platform are comparable to or better than those reported in recent literatures (please refer to Table S1 and Table S2).

Because dimethoate was detected via the use of CDs as fluorescent probes following the catalytic reaction, it was crucial to investigate the influence of phenol and the related product of the oxidative coupling reaction on the detection process. The results indicated that the catalysis of the coupling reaction with phenol concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 µM did not lead to significant changes in the emission intensity of the CD probe (see Fig. 5 A) or in the fluorescence signal (ΔF) induced by 0.1 µg/mL dimethoate (see Fig. 5B). The results reveal that presence of phenol did not affect the detection of dimethoate.

Selectivity is another critical parameter for evaluating the analytical performance of the platform. In the context of phenol detection, potential interferences included 3 mM Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, Na+, K+, Li+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, methionine, glycine, sarcosine, acetone, cyclohexane, ethanol, acetonitrile, dimethoate, catechol and hydroquinone, all of which were present at concentrations 100-fold greater than that of phenol (30 µM). As illustrated in Fig. 5C, inorganic ions and non-phenolic compounds did not interfere with the detection of phenol. However, a 100-fold increase in catechol and hydroquinone, which contain phenolic groups in their structures, did influence detection. The oxidative coupling of phenolic compounds with 4-AAP has been extensively studied and widely applied. It is well established that the structures of phenolic compounds and the physicochemical properties of the catalysts determine the reaction rates and ultimately the yields of the coupling products [51]. Through a careful review of the literature, we found that the bimetallic doping nanomaterials Fe,Cu-CDs used in this work exhibit higher selectivity against catechol and hydroquinone in detecting phenol compared to other Cu-based nanomaterials, such as DNA-copper hybrid nanoflowers [52] and amorphous metal–organic framework (MOF)-based Cu-nanozymes [53].

The selectivity of the system toward dimethoate was further assessed using Na+, K+, Li+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, methionine, glycine, sarcosine, cartap, cypermethrin, fenvalerate, and thiamethoxam as potential interferents, at concentrations of 10 mM each, which were approximately 2.3 × 104 times greater than that of dimethoate (0.1 µg/mL, 0.43 µM). Fig. 5D demonstrates that a more pronounced fluorescence change was observed upon the addition of dimethoate, which can be attributed to the specific interaction between dimethoate and AChE.

In this study, bimetallic oxidase-mimetic fluorescent Fe,Cu-CDs were synthesized and employed to develop a dual-mode sensing platform for the detection of phenol and dimethoate. The oxidase-like activity of the CDs facilitated the catalysis of the oxidative coupling reaction between phenol and 4-AAP, resulting in a pronounced absorbance at 502 nm. Furthermore, within the AChE-ATCh system, the interaction between the Cu in the CDs and the sulfhydryl groups of the hydrolysis product TCh led to a significant increase in fluorescence. By leveraging the inhibitory effect of OPs on AChE activity, this method enables the quantitative detection of dimethoate. The method offered excellent sensitivity, good reproducibility, and an acceptable recovery rate. The results suggest that the as-prepared dual-functional Fe,Cu-CDs are promising materials for the rapid detection of phenol and dimethoate in environmental samples and agricultural products.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125794.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of phenols in waters using micro-pumped multicommutation and spectrophotometric detection: an automated alternative to the standard procedure
Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Ródenas-Torralba, A. Morales-Rubio, M. de la Guardia, Determination of phenols in waters using micro-pumped multicommutation and spectrophotometric detection: an automated alternative to the standard procedure, Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry. 383 (2005) 138-144. 10.1007/s00216-005-3416-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shell-isolated nanoparticle-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Nature
